findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who need to solve it — add a slide showing 3-4 specific customer conversations (even brief ones) that validate the pain points you're describing, like "spoke with 12 villa developers in Dubai who cited corrosion and storm damage as reasons they're hesitant to install solar hot water systems" or "collected letters of intent from 2 major GCC developers interested in piloting a more reliable system." This doesn't need to be a massive study — just evidence that real customers are frustrated enough to engage with you.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like background noise—add a timeline that shows the urgency is *now*. For example, add a callout like "Estidama Phase 3 enforcement begins Q3 2025 — 40% of new villa permits now require solar hot water systems" or "UAE Climate Law targets 30% renewable energy by 2030, creating 18-month window before stricter penalties kick in." This transforms "regulations are tightening" into "your window to capture this mandated market closes in X months if you don't move."

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature wishlist—you're describing what it will do, not how it works or why it's hard to replicate. Add one concrete technical detail that shows you've actually solved a real problem: for example, "Our proprietary algorithm accounts for seasonal demand variation and hard-water scaling in GCC climates—most generic solar sizing tools ignore both, leading to 20-30% undersizing in our region" or "We've built a thermal modeling engine that predicts performance across 50+ building archetypes using real deployment data from Rixos and pilot sites, giving us accuracy competitors can't match without similar operational history." This transforms HelioHelp from "

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as your scaling engine, but investors will want to know what's underneath it—specifically, which cloud platforms, APIs, or third-party AI services power the system (AWS, Google Cloud, OpenAI, etc.) and whether any of those dependencies could create bottlenecks or cost surprises as you scale. Add a brief "Technology Stack" section to Slide 19 listing your core infrastructure dependencies and a one-liner on how you're mitigating lock-in risk—for example: "HelioHelp runs on AWS with containerized architecture enabling multi-cloud portability" or "Uses open-source LLM framework to avoid vendor dependency."

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, EU) with a product that touches building codes and utility mandates — add one bullet to Slide 5 (Technology) under HelioPro stating something like "Compliant with UAE.AE building standards, EU solar thermal directives, and local utility interconnection requirements" so investors see you've thought through the regulatory guardrails, not just the tech. This matters especially since your Slide 16 mentions "regulatory tailwinds" but never actually names what you're compliant with.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides show financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a one-slide assumptions sheet that breaks down your 2026–2030 projections into three core drivers: (1) unit volume growth—show how you get from 50 installations in 2026 to your 2030 targets (e.g., "50 units 2026 → 150 units 2027 via 3 developer partnerships → 400 units 2028 as ALDAR scales"), (2) average selling price per installation, and (3) cost of goods sold per unit—for example, "Hardware COGS: $8,500/unit in 2026, declining to $6,200/unit by 2028 through

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and the Rixos payback period, but investors need to see CAC and LTV to understand how efficiently you're acquiring customers—especially as you scale from direct sales to the partner channel. Add a simple table showing: for hotels/resorts, what's your average deal size ($207K for Rixos), estimated customer acquisition cost (sales/marketing spend per deal), and payback period in months; then do the same for the villa segment using your EtihadWE pilot ($6K per villa, but likely bundled in developer deals). This lets investors see whether your unit economics hold when you're not selling directly.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy of $87,000 between the contracted amount and projected hardware revenue.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue shows $0.3M, but Slide 12 only has $213K in contracted deals—that's a $87K gap that investors will immediately spot and question your forecast credibility. Clarify whether the $0.3M includes pipeline deals beyond the two named customers (like the strategic discussions with Abu Dhabi Ports or ALDAR), and if so, add a footnote on Slide 14 breaking down: Contracted $213K + Pipeline conversion assumption $87K = $0.3M total, so investors understand your math isn't loose.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy this new funding round—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing scale-up: $X to expand Ras Al Khaimah facility," "Sales & partnerships: $X for developer relationships," "Working capital: $X for inventory ahead of Q2 2026 deployments") with specific dollar amounts and timelines tied to your pipeline milestones. Right now investors see your $213K contracted revenue and $350K seed runway, but they don't know what this new raise is for or how it gets you to the next inflection point.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets that confirms payment status — something like "Rixos (live project, $X revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, first payment received [date])". Right now investors can't tell if these are installed systems generating actual cash or still in deployment, so one concrete data point per customer transforms this from "promising pilots" to "proven revenue."

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: You've got great revenue streams mapped out, but investors need to see actual growth momentum—add specific numbers showing how revenue or units have grown month-over-month or year-over-year (for example, "Revenue grew from $120K in Jan to $280K in March" or "Units sold increased 45% MoM from Q1 to Q2"). This proves your model is working, not just theoretically sound.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos who stuck with you through a severe storm—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to your Rixos case study: something like "100% system uptime post-deployment" or "zero service calls in 14 months of operation" or whatever your actual retention/reliability number is. This proves your product doesn't just win deals, it keeps customers happy and reduces churn risk, which is exactly what investors worry about in hardware.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Replace "Legacy Solar," "Heat Pumps," and "Electric Storage" with actual competitor names—something like "Sunrun Solar," "Carrier Heat Pumps," and "Tesla Powerwall"—so investors immediately understand who you're beating and can validate your claims against real products they know. Right now the table reads as theoretical; naming competitors makes it credible and memorable.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing products, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Daikin, or a regional conglomerate decides to build a flat-roof solution—add a brief section explaining your defensibility, something like "Why incumbents can't easily copy us: our polymer absorber patent (filed Q2 2024) + 18-month lead time to certify for Gulf storm standards means even well-funded competitors face 2+ years to market parity." This transforms your feature advantages into structural moats that matter to investors.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing the connective tissue that makes investors confident in execution—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who fill gaps and bring credibility in areas your team is still building (like fundraising, scaling manufacturing, or entering new markets). For example, if you're scaling from prototype to production, name an advisor who's successfully scaled solar or cleantech manufacturing; if you're targeting enterprise customers, add someone with that sales network. This shows you're not betting everything on your core team alone.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for founders and key hires—something like "Founder equity: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff; key team members vest over 3-4 years post-funding." Investors will ask this in every meeting anyway, so getting ahead of it shows you've thought through retention and alignment, and it prevents them from assuming the worst (like everyone's fully vested already).

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification mentioned on Slide 3, which is great, but investors in regulated markets need to see the full compliance picture upfront—add a dedicated compliance slide before the financials that maps out which specific UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai Municipality, Barjeel) your system meets, what certifications you already hold versus what you're pursuing by when, and crucially, whether you need any additional electrical or plumbing permits for residential installations. For example: "Solar Keymark ✓ | Estidama compliance pathway (Q2 2026) | Dubai Municipality electrical approval (in progress, expected Q3 2026) | Local installer licensing requirements (

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down and a solid risk mitigation section, but investors will want to see explicit language confirming you have zero pending IP disputes, patent challenges, or licensing conflicts—especially since you're claiming patented technology as a core moat and licensing is part of your revenue model. Add a single sentence to your IP section (Slide 8 or 3) stating something like: "All patents are unencumbered, no pending disputes or third-party claims exist, and freedom-to-operate has been confirmed across US, EU, and UK jurisdictions"—this removes ambiguity and signals you've done your legal homework before they ask.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these match. However, Slide 6 states '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' which would imply a $400M total market, but also states 'UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment' as the SAM. If 5% of $400M = $20M, this is internally consistent. No clear numerical contradiction found across slides.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete slide deck
findings.suggestion: Go through the entire deck systematically and replace every instance of [URL_REDACTED] and [UNKNOWN] with actual content — Slide 3's title should read "Solution" or "Product" (not [UNKNOWN]), and every URL placeholder needs the real link or be removed entirely. This takes 30 minutes but signals to investors that your deck is final and investor-ready, not a work-in-progress template.
